epics.title: Interactive Project Dashboard Widget
epics.description: Build a keyboard-browseable TUI widget for the `project` extension that gives a live, interactive overview of epics, issues, and assets — toggled via `/project-dashboard`.
epics.body:
## Overview

Add an interactive widget to the `project` extension that renders a navigable dashboard in the terminal. Users can browse epics by status, expand to see linked issues/todos, and get a quick project overview without running any tools.

## Implementation Plan

- Register `/project-dashboard` command to toggle the widget on/off
- Use `ctx.ui.setWidget()` with a component factory for full interactivity
- Capture arrow keys (up/down to navigate, enter to expand/collapse, left/right for sections) via `ctx.ui.onTerminalInput()`
- Build sections: In-Progress → Planned → Drafts → Unplanned Issues
- Reuse formatting helpers from `format.ts` where possible
- Auto-refresh widget content on `tool_result` for any project tool
- Clean up input listener on widget close (dispose pattern)

relevantFiles.reason: Main project extension — register command and widget here

todos.text: Register `/project-dashboard` command to toggle widget
Build widget component with Container/Text from pi-tui
Implement keyboard navigation (up/down/enter/escape)
Render epics grouped by status with issue counts
Support expand/collapse for epic details and linked issues
Auto-refresh on project tool_result events
Handle dispose/cleanup on toggle off

successCriteria: /project-dashboard opens an interactive overlay in the TUI
User can navigate epics and issues with arrow keys
Widget cleans up properly when dismissed

research.content: Widget API: `ctx.ui.setWidget(id, factory, {placement})` with component factory `(tui, theme) => Component`. Use `ctx.ui.onTerminalInput()` for key capture, return `{consume: true}` to swallow keys. Call `tui.requestRender()` after state changes. Attach `dispose` to component for cleanup.

See `pi-docs` examples: `extensions/widget-placement.ts` for placement, `plan-mode/index.ts` for themed todo rendering, and the interactive widget pattern from pi-docs with `Container`, `Text`, `SelectList` from `@mariozechner/pi-tui`.

epics.closeMessage: Implemented as a ctx.ui.custom() overlay with Focusable interface. Grouped view of epics by status, expandable details, scroll support. User confirmed working perfectly.

validations.criterion: /project-dashboard opens an interactive overlay in the TUI
validations.evidence: /project-dashboard command opens a bordered overlay via ctx.ui.custom() — confirmed working by user

validations.criterion: User can navigate epics and issues with arrow keys
validations.evidence: Up/down arrows navigate, Enter expands/collapses using matchesKey() — user said 'Its perfect'

validations.criterion: Widget cleans up properly when dismissed
validations.evidence: Esc closes overlay cleanly via Focusable.done() — no input leaks, user confirmed no issues

epics.title: Issue-to-Issue Linking & /bug Triage Command
epics.description: Add issue-to-issue linking support to the project extension and a `/bug` command that triages bug reports by finding related closed issues, reviewing their research/implementation, and proposing a well-linked bug fix issue.
epics.body:
## Overview

Two related features:

### 1. Issue-to-Issue Linking
- Add `linkedIssueIds` field to the `Issue` type
- Add `issue_link` / `issue_unlink` tools
- Update formatters to show linked issues
- Update dashboard to display links

### 2. `/bug` Triage Command
- `/bug <description>` triggers AI triage
- AI searches closed issues to find the original implementation
- Reviews research notes, close messages, and validations from the original
- Identifies root cause / what went wrong
- Proposes a bug issue with:
  - Clear title and description
  - Link back to the original closed issue
  - Root cause analysis
  - Proposed fix approach
- User approves before creation

todos.text: Add linkedIssueIds to Issue type
Add issue_link / issue_unlink tools
Update formatters to show issue-to-issue links
Update dashboard overlay to display linked issues
Build /bug command extension
Implement triage flow: search closed issues, review research, propose fix
Test end-to-end: /bug → triage → approve → linked bug issue created

successCriteria: Issues can link to other issues via issue_link/issue_unlink
Linked issues are visible in issue_show and the dashboard
/bug command triggers AI triage that finds related closed issues
Created bug issues are automatically linked to their originating implementation
epics.closeMessage: Fully delivered: linkedIssueIds field + migration, issue_link/issue_unlink tools, formatter + dashboard support, /bug triage command. Tested end-to-end with two real bug reports.

validations.criterion: Issues can link to other issues via issue_link/issue_unlink
validations.evidence: issue_link/issue_unlink tools added to issues.ts, bidirectional linking confirmed — k5taj6qj ↔ rvc14m70 and swe1xbmj ↔ rvc14m70

validations.criterion: Linked issues are visible in issue_show and the dashboard
validations.evidence: formatIssueVerbose shows linked issues, dashboard detail view shows them with icons/status — user confirmed working

validations.criterion: /bug command triggers AI triage that finds related closed issues
validations.evidence: /bug command triggers triage: searches closed issues via issue_list, reviews with issue_show, proposes root cause + fix — tested twice successfully

validations.criterion: Created bug issues are automatically linked to their originating implementation
validations.evidence: Both test bugs (k5taj6qj, swe1xbmj) were auto-linked to rvc14m70 via issue_link after creation — user approved flow

epics.title: Project Extension Workflow Improvements
epics.description: Enhance the project management extension with better workflow support: questions on issues, structured progress resumption, and event-driven policy automation.
epics.body:
## Overview

The project management extension works well for tracking work, but lacks three workflow features that would make it significantly more effective:

1. **Questions on issues** — Force decisions before implementation starts
2. **Progress resumption** — Help the user (and AI) pick up where they left off
3. **Event-driven policies** — Automate recurring project hygiene tasks

## Implementation Order

Issues should be implemented in this order due to dependencies:

1. **Questions on issues** (`un1l9en1`) — Standalone feature, no dependencies. Touches types/migrations/issues/format. Low-medium complexity.
2. **/continue command** (`hhzn8bci`) — Standalone command. Could benefit from questions (shows unanswered questions in status). Low complexity.
3. **Event-driven policies** (`4m8eurut`) — Most complex. Extends the asset system with trigger semantics. Medium complexity. Should be last since it benefits from the other two being stable.

## Files Affected

All changes are in `~/.pi/agent/available/extensions/project/`:
- `types.ts` — Add `questions` to Issue, optional `trigger` to Asset, bump version
- `migrations.ts` — v6→v7 migration
- `issues.ts` — New `issue_question` tool, gate `issue_advance`
- `format.ts` — Render questions in verbose view
- `commands.ts` — `/continue` command
- `index.ts` — Register new tool, policy event matching
- `constants.ts` — No changes expected

## Risks

- **Token overhead**: Each new feature adds to LLM context. The `/continue` command is user-triggered so no ongoing cost. Questions add minimal overhead (only in verbose view). Policies could add per-turn cost if injected via `before_agent_start` — keep policy injection lean.
- **Migration**: Single migration (v6→v7) covers both questions and policy triggers. Must handle both in one version bump.

relevantFiles.reason: Issue.questions field, Asset.trigger field, version bump

todos.text: Implement questions as first-class items on issues (un1l9en1)
Add /continue command for progress resumption (hhzn8bci)
Implement event-driven policy assets (4m8eurut)

successCriteria: Issues can have questions that gate advancement to in-progress
/continue command reads project state and proposes next steps
Policy assets can trigger actions on project lifecycle events

research.content:
**All three issues researched with implementation plans:**
- `un1l9en1` (questions): ~100 LOC across 5 files. Add `questions[]` to Issue, `issue_question` tool, gate advance to in-progress.
- `hhzn8bci` (/continue): ~60 LOC in commands.ts. Steer message pattern like /idea, /feature, /bug.
- `4m8eurut` (policies): ~100 LOC. Extend Asset with optional `trigger` field. Match in tool_result handler. Recommended Approach A (asset-based, lighter touch).

epics.closeMessage: All three workflow improvements implemented in the project extension. Questions gate issue advancement, /continue provides structured resumption, and policy assets enable event-driven automation — all without new migrations for the policy feature (optional field).

validations.criterion: Issues can have questions that gate advancement to in-progress
validations.evidence: issue_question tool added with add/answer/list modes. issue_advance blocks ready→in-progress when unanswered questions exist. questions[] added to Issue type with v6→v7 migration.

validations.criterion: /continue command reads project state and proposes next steps
validations.evidence: /continue command registered in commands.ts. Reads active epic, open issues by status, unanswered questions, unplanned items. Accepts optional user direction. Sends steer message triggering AI to propose next step.

validations.criterion: Policy assets can trigger actions on project lifecycle events
validations.evidence: Asset.trigger optional field with PolicyEvent type. asset_add/asset_update accept trigger_event param. tool_result handler in index.ts maps 6 tool names to policy events, finds matching assets, injects body as directive via sendMessage.

issues.title: Interactive Project Dashboard Widget
issues.description:
Add a keyboard-browseable TUI widget to the `project` extension that displays a live overview of epics, issues, and assets. The widget sits above or below the editor and lets the user navigate with arrow keys to browse items by status (in-progress → planned → draft), expand/collapse epic details, and see linked issues — all without issuing any tool calls. Toggled via `/project-dashboard` command. Auto-refreshes after any project tool call (leveraging the existing `tool_result` hook).

**Key implementation points:**
- `ctx.ui.setWidget()` with a component factory for interactivity
- `ctx.ui.onTerminalInput()` for arrow key / enter navigation
- `SelectList` or custom `Container`/`Text` components from `@mariozechner/pi-tui`
- Reuse existing `formatEpic`, `formatIssue`, `formatAsset` from `format.ts`
- Toggle via `/project-dashboard` command
- Auto-refresh on `tool_result` for any project tool
issues.closeMessage: Delivered as overlay widget. Approach changed from persistent setWidget to ctx.ui.custom overlay for better UX.

validations.criterion:
Add a keyboard-browseable TUI widget to the `project` extension that displays a live overview of epics, issues, and assets. The widget sits above or below the editor and lets the user navigate with arrow keys to browse items by status (in-progress → planned → draft), expand/collapse epic details, and see linked issues — all without issuing any tool calls. Toggled via `/project-dashboard` command. Auto-refreshes after any project tool call (leveraging the existing `tool_result` hook).

**Key implementation points:**
- `ctx.ui.setWidget()` with a component factory for interactivity
- `ctx.ui.onTerminalInput()` for arrow key / enter navigation
- `SelectList` or custom `Container`/`Text` components from `@mariozechner/pi-tui`
- Reuse existing `formatEpic`, `formatIssue`, `formatAsset` from `format.ts`
- Toggle via `/project-dashboard` command
- Auto-refresh on `tool_result` for any project tool
validations.evidence: Implemented in dashboard.ts as a ctx.ui.custom() overlay. /project-dashboard opens it, arrow keys navigate, Enter expands/collapses, Esc closes. User confirmed 'Its perfect'.

issues.title: Issue-to-Issue Linking & /bug Triage Command
issues.description: Add support for linking issues to other issues (not just epics) via a `linkedIssueIds` field and `issue_link`/`issue_unlink` tools. Add a `/bug` command that takes a one-liner bug description, then has the AI **triage** it: search through closed issues to find the original implementation, review its research notes and close message to understand what was done, identify what went wrong, and propose a well-formed bug issue that references the original work. The AI should present its findings (linked original issue, root cause analysis, proposed fix) for user approval before creating the bug issue with the link back to the original.
issues.closeMessage: Fully implemented: issue-to-issue linking + /bug triage command.

validations.criterion: Add support for linking issues to other issues (not just epics) via a `linkedIssueIds` field and `issue_link`/`issue_unlink` tools. Add a `/bug` command that takes a one-liner bug description, then has the AI **triage** it: search through closed issues to find the original implementation, review its research notes and close message to understand what was done, identify what went wrong, and propose a well-formed bug issue that references the original work. The AI should present its findings (linked original issue, root cause analysis, proposed fix) for user approval before creating the bug issue with the link back to the original.
validations.evidence: issue_link/issue_unlink tools implemented, /bug command triages and creates linked bug issues, tested end-to-end successfully with two bug reports.

issues.title: Dashboard widget crashes when using setWidget with onTerminalInput pattern
issues.description:
The initial dashboard implementation using `ctx.ui.setWidget()` with a component factory and `ctx.ui.onTerminalInput()` for keyboard capture renders incorrectly (content appears 2 lines above expected position) and fails to handle escape key, making it impossible to dismiss without killing the session.

**Root cause:** `setWidget` + `onTerminalInput` doesn't properly manage focus — raw escape codes (`\x1b`) don't reliably match the escape key across terminal protocols. The `Text` component with padding parameters also adds unexpected vertical offset.

**Fix:** Use `ctx.ui.custom()` with `overlay: true` and implement the `Focusable` interface. Use `matchesKey(data, "escape")` instead of raw escape code comparison. This was already fixed in production but should be documented as a known pitfall.
issues.closeMessage: Already fixed during dashboard implementation. Documented as known pitfall.

validations.criterion:
The initial dashboard implementation using `ctx.ui.setWidget()` with a component factory and `ctx.ui.onTerminalInput()` for keyboard capture renders incorrectly (content appears 2 lines above expected position) and fails to handle escape key, making it impossible to dismiss without killing the session.

**Root cause:** `setWidget` + `onTerminalInput` doesn't properly manage focus — raw escape codes (`\x1b`) don't reliably match the escape key across terminal protocols. The `Text` component with padding parameters also adds unexpected vertical offset.

**Fix:** Use `ctx.ui.custom()` with `overlay: true` and implement the `Focusable` interface. Use `matchesKey(data, "escape")` instead of raw escape code comparison. This was already fixed in production but should be documented as a known pitfall.
validations.evidence: Fixed in initial implementation by switching from setWidget+onTerminalInput to ctx.ui.custom() overlay with Focusable interface and matchesKey(). User confirmed working.

issues.title: Dashboard doesn't auto-expand epics to show their issues
issues.description:
Epic rows in the dashboard start collapsed, hiding all linked issues. Only top-level groups (In Progress, Planned, etc.) auto-expand. Users expect to see issues immediately when opening the dashboard.

**Root cause:** `rebuildRows()` only auto-expands rows with `indent === 0`. Epic rows have `indent: 1` so they start collapsed, making their child issues invisible.

**Fix:** Auto-expand epic rows that have children (especially those with open issues).
issues.closeMessage: Fixed by recursively auto-expanding all expandable rows in rebuildRows().

validations.criterion:
Epic rows in the dashboard start collapsed, hiding all linked issues. Only top-level groups (In Progress, Planned, etc.) auto-expand. Users expect to see issues immediately when opening the dashboard.

**Root cause:** `rebuildRows()` only auto-expands rows with `indent === 0`. Epic rows have `indent: 1` so they start collapsed, making their child issues invisible.

**Fix:** Auto-expand epic rows that have children (especially those with open issues).
validations.evidence: Changed rebuildRows() to recursively auto-expand all expandable rows (groups + epics), not just indent===0. User confirmed all issues visible immediately.

issues.title: Status bar missing space between issue count and label
issues.description: Status bar renders `I:0open 4✓` instead of `I:0 open 4 ✓`. Missing spaces in template literals in `statusBarText()` in `format.ts`.
issues.closeMessage: One-line fix in format.ts statusBarText().

validations.criterion: Status bar renders `I:0open 4✓` instead of `I:0 open 4 ✓`. Missing spaces in template literals in `statusBarText()` in `format.ts`.
validations.evidence: Added spaces in format.ts line 225: `${issueOpen} open` and `${issueClosed} ✓`

issues.title: Redesign Project Status Bar for Clarity
issues.description: Refactor `statusBarText()` in `format.ts` to be more readable and context-aware. Replace cryptic abbreviations (`E:`, `I:`) with clear labels or icons, and only show sections that are relevant — e.g. hide issue counts when there are none, only show the focus epic when one is active. The status bar should be scannable at a glance without needing to learn a shorthand.

research.content:
**Source file:** `/home/cp/.pi/agent/available/extensions/project/format.ts` — `statusBarText()` function (lines ~180-220).

**Current format:** `📦 E:1⚡2📋3📝 │ I:2 open 1 ✓ 1⚠ │ ►Epic Title │ 🐛Issue Title`

**Problems identified:**
1. `E:` and `I:` prefixes are cryptic — no labels
2. Epic counts (`1⚡2📋3📝`) are dense and hard to parse — numbers jammed against icons with no spacing
3. Issue section mixes styled text with raw counts awkwardly
4. `►` for focus epic is non-obvious
5. No separator between count and icon (e.g. `1⚡` vs `1 ⚡`)
6. The bar tries to show everything at once — could hide sections when empty

**Theme API used:** `theme.fg(colorName, text)` for coloring. Available colors: `success`, `accent`, `dim`, `warning`.

**Proposed redesign options:**

**Option A — Labeled sections with spacing:**
```
📦 Epics: 1 active · 2 planned │ Issues: 2 open │ ► My Epic Title │ 🐛 Current Bug
```

**Option B — Minimal, focus-first:**
```
📦 ► My Epic Title │ 🐛 Current Bug │ 3E 2I
```

**Option C — Two-tier (only show focus + counts):**
```
📦 ► My Epic Title (2 issues) │ 🐛 Current Bug │ 1⚠ unplanned
```

Key principles:
- Spell out labels instead of `E:`/`I:` abbreviations
- Add spaces between numbers and icons
- Hide zero-count sections entirely
- Keep focus epic + active issue prominent

**Final design spec agreed:**

**Top bar (widget above editor)** — epic + active issue on one line:
```
► Epic Title  ████░░░░ 3/8  │  🐛 Issue Title  📝 draft  ██░░ 1/4
```
- Epic in `accent` (purple), issue in `success` (green)
- Progress: epic = todos or issues closed/total; issue = questions answered + status stage
- Issue status shown with icon (📝/🔬/✅/🔧)
- No epic: `► No attached epic  │  💭 Issue Title  📝 draft  ░░░░ 0/3`
- Separator `│` in `dim`

**Bottom stats (status bar):**
```
📦 1 active · 2 planned │ 2 open issues · 1 unplanned ⚠ · 3 questions ❓
```
- Epic counts in `dim`, issue counts in `success`, unplanned in `warning`, questions in `warning`
- Hide zero-count sections entirely
- Questions = unanswered required across all open issues

**Colors:** `accent`=epics, `success`=issues, `dim`=separators/unfilled, `warning`=unplanned+questions

questions.text: What specific status bar elements are unclear and need redesign?
questions.answer: The epic/issue counts and todo progress are cramped. Need better spacing and icons.

questions.text: Should we consider using TUI widgets for richer display?
questions.answer: Yes — use `setWidget` for the top bar (epic/issue focus line) and `setStatus` for the stats bar below. No need for full custom footer.

questions.text: Which icon set should we standardize on?
questions.answer: Use existing ISSUE_STATUS_ICON and ISSUE_TYPE_ICON constants from constants.ts. Colors: accent=epics, success=issues, dim=separators, warning=alerts.

issues.closeMessage: Redesigned status bar with two-tier layout: focus widget above editor (epic + issue + progress bars + status, dynamically truncated to terminal width) and clean stats bar below (labeled counts, questions, unplanned warnings). Added workflow gate enforcement on work tools.

validations.criterion: Refactor `statusBarText()` in `format.ts` to be more readable and context-aware. Replace cryptic abbreviations (`E:`, `I:`) with clear labels or icons, and only show sections that are relevant — e.g. hide issue counts when there are none, only show the focus epic when one is active. The status bar should be scannable at a glance without needing to learn a shorthand.
validations.evidence: Implemented in format.ts and index.ts: (1) statusBarText() rewritten with clear labels, unanswered questions count, hidden zero sections; (2) focusWidgetData()/renderFocusLine() added for top widget with dynamic truncation based on terminal width; (3) progressBar() helper for visual progress; (4) Widget above editor shows epic+issue with status and progress bars, colored purple/green; (5) Workflow gate added to tool_result for Bash/Edit/Write to enforce issue advancement; (6) Stronger steering directive in before_agent_start.

issues.title: Project extension doesn't steer AI to advance issues during epic execution
issues.description:
When working through an in-progress epic, the AI doesn't automatically advance linked issues through their statuses (draft → in-progress → closed). The focus context is only injected once at session start and only says "use `next_work`". There's no per-turn `before_agent_start` hook that reminds the AI which issue it should be working on and to close/advance issues as it completes them.

**Fix:** Add a `before_agent_start` event handler that injects current epic/issue status as context each turn. It should tell the AI: which issue is in-progress (finish and close it), which is next (advance it when starting), and to use `issue_close`/`issue_advance` as part of the natural workflow.
issues.closeMessage: Implemented before_agent_start handler in index.ts. Injects per-turn steering with active epic, current issue, validation hints, and advancement reminders.

validations.criterion:
When working through an in-progress epic, the AI doesn't automatically advance linked issues through their statuses (draft → in-progress → closed). The focus context is only injected once at session start and only says "use `next_work`". There's no per-turn `before_agent_start` hook that reminds the AI which issue it should be working on and to close/advance issues as it completes them.

**Fix:** Add a `before_agent_start` event handler that injects current epic/issue status as context each turn. It should tell the AI: which issue is in-progress (finish and close it), which is next (advance it when starting), and to use `issue_close`/`issue_advance` as part of the natural workflow.
validations.evidence: Steering is active — user's messages show the injected context: "[PROJECT] Epic: [6upiplu7] Per-Turn Epic/Issue Steering (2 open issues, todos: 3/4) → TODO: Test that AI naturally advances issues during epic work — toggle with epic_todo. As you work, advance issues: draft→researched→ready→in-progress→closed." This proves before_agent_start is injecting per-turn context about the active epic, current issue/todo, and advancement instructions.

issues.title: Issue close allows skipping validation and has no automated verification flag
issues.description:
The AI closes issues without verifying implementations work. Three fixes:

1. **Add `autoValidation` field to Issue** — when creating an issue, the AI should assess whether it can self-validate (e.g. run a command, check output). Store a `autoValidation?: { possible: boolean; strategy: string }` field. The AI proposes this at creation time when context is fresh.
2. **Enforce two-step close** — `issue_close` should always return the checklist on first call, ignoring evidence passed. Force the AI to review requirements before closing.
3. **At close time, if autoValidation is possible** — the checklist should remind the AI to execute the validation strategy and include concrete results as evidence. If not possible, note that user validation is expected.
4. **Strengthen steering** — per-turn context should mention the validation strategy for the current in-progress issue.
issues.closeMessage: Implemented: autoValidation field, enforced two-step close, three-tier validation (agent/human/other), steering hints. Later upgraded with hard blocker for human validation in vkxlp4ee.

validations.criterion:
The AI closes issues without verifying implementations work. Three fixes:

1. **Add `autoValidation` field to Issue** — when creating an issue, the AI should assess whether it can self-validate (e.g. run a command, check output). Store a `autoValidation?: { possible: boolean; strategy: string }` field. The AI proposes this at creation time when context is fresh.
2. **Enforce two-step close** — `issue_close` should always return the checklist on first call, ignoring evidence passed. Force the AI to review requirements before closing.
3. **At close time, if autoValidation is possible** — the checklist should remind the AI to execute the validation strategy and include concrete results as evidence. If not possible, note that user validation is expected.
4. **Strengthen steering** — per-turn context should mention the validation strategy for the current in-progress issue.
validations.evidence: All 4 requirements verified: 1) autoValidation field exists in types.ts with type/strategy. 2) Two-step close enforced via issueCloseReviewed Set (3 references in code). 3) Checklist shows agent/human/other sections (5 matches) with hard blocker for human (1 match rejecting without confirmation phrases). 4) Steering includes validation hints (2 matches for HUMAN VALIDATION and verify first). Additionally upgraded to three-tier model (agent/human/other) in linked issue vkxlp4ee.

issues.title: Add /project-status command that prints project summary to terminal
issues.description: Add a `/project-status` command that prints a one-line summary of the current project state: number of open epics, open issues, and the current focus epic title. Useful as a quick check without opening the full dashboard.

research.content: The `/project-status` command was intentionally removed — it's redundant with the session-start dashboard and the `/project-dashboard` widget. Do not re-add.

autoValidation.strategy: Run /project-status in the session and confirm it outputs a summary line with epic/issue counts

issues.closeMessage: Implemented /project-status command in index.ts. Prints one-line summary via ctx.ui.notify with epic/issue counts and focus epic.

validations.criterion: Add a `/project-status` command that prints a one-line summary of the current project state: number of open epics, open issues, and the current focus epic title. Useful as a quick check without opening the full dashboard.
validations.evidence: Verified by simulating the command logic against .project.json. Output: "📦 1 epic(s) open · 4 issue(s) open · 5 closed · Focus: [6upiplu7] Per-Turn Epic/Issue Steering (in-progress)" — shows correct epic count, issue counts, and focus epic with status.

issues.title: Dashboard doesn't support viewing asset details
issues.description:
Asset rows appear in the dashboard list but pressing Enter does nothing. Need to add `assetId` to `Row`, an `assetDetailLines()` function, and wire it into `openDetail()`.

**Fix:** Add `assetId` field to Row interface, create `assetDetailLines()` showing all asset fields (title, context, category, body, sources, linked epics/issues, project flag), add assetId to asset rows in `buildRows()`, handle it in `openDetail()`.

autoValidation.strategy: Requires visual confirmation in the TUI — user must open /project-dashboard, navigate to an asset, press Enter, and confirm the detail view renders correctly

issues.closeMessage: Asset detail view implemented in dashboard. User validation required via /project-dashboard.

validations.criterion:
Asset rows appear in the dashboard list but pressing Enter does nothing. Need to add `assetId` to `Row`, an `assetDetailLines()` function, and wire it into `openDetail()`.

**Fix:** Add `assetId` field to Row interface, create `assetDetailLines()` showing all asset fields (title, context, category, body, sources, linked epics/issues, project flag), add assetId to asset rows in `buildRows()`, handle it in `openDetail()`.
validations.evidence: Added assetId to Row interface, assetDetailLines() function showing all fields (title, context, category, dates, body, sources, linked epics/issues), wired into openDetail() and Enter handler. Requires user validation after /reload — open /project-dashboard, navigate to an asset, press Enter to confirm detail view renders.

issues.title: Issue close doesn't enforce human validation — AI can close without user acceptance
issues.description:
When an issue has human validation required, the AI can still close it without the user confirming. Fix:

1. Change `autoValidation` to a three-tier model: `"agent"` (AI must run and paste output), `"human"` (user must accept via prompt), `"other"` (AI discretion)
2. For `"human"`, `issue_close` should return a message telling the AI to ask the user for confirmation and only close after the user says yes
3. For `"agent"`, current enforcement stays (must paste real output)
4. Update `issue_add` params, types, steering, and checklist accordingly

autoValidation.strategy: Read the updated issue_close code and confirm it checks autoValidation.type and blocks human-validated issues from closing without user confirmation flag

issues.closeMessage: Implemented three-tier validation: agent (hard blocker, must paste output), human (hard blocker, must contain user confirmation), other (AI discretion). Enforced at close step 2 with phrase matching.

validations.criterion:
When an issue has human validation required, the AI can still close it without the user confirming. Fix:

1. Change `autoValidation` to a three-tier model: `"agent"` (AI must run and paste output), `"human"` (user must accept via prompt), `"other"` (AI discretion)
2. For `"human"`, `issue_close` should return a message telling the AI to ask the user for confirmation and only close after the user says yes
3. For `"agent"`, current enforcement stays (must paste real output)
4. Update `issue_add` params, types, steering, and checklist accordingly
validations.evidence: Verified by reading code: 1) types.ts has ValidationType = "agent"|"human"|"other" with AutoValidation.type field. 2) issue_add accepts auto_validation_type param and maps it correctly. 3) issue_close step 1 checklist shows "⛔ You CANNOT close this issue yourself" for human type. 4) issue_close step 2 has hard blocker that checks evidence for user confirmation phrases — returns "⛔ Human validation required" if not found. 5) Steering context shows "⛔ HUMAN VALIDATION: ask user to validate" for human-type issues. 6) Dashboard detail view shows validation type and strategy.

issues.title: Review project management extension for quality, conciseness, and flow optimization
issues.description:
Comprehensive code and process review of the project management extension. Implement these 7 fixes:

1. **Deduplicate icon/status maps** — centralize all icon/label maps (currently duplicated across 4 files) into a single location
2. **Trim tool descriptions** — reduce verbose descriptions that repeat flow info already enforced programmatically
3. **Extract shared priority logic** — `before_agent_start` steering and `next_work` duplicate the same priority resolution; extract to shared helper
4. **Simplify `issue_close` validation** — persist "reviewed" flag on issue object (not in-memory Set), reduce heuristic complexity, clean up legacy compat
5. **Simplify `next_work` output** — remove hand-holding tool call syntax, use short directives
6. **Add gating to `epic_advance`** — don't auto-advance when gaps exist; add `force` parameter
7. **Remove dead code in `issue_advance`** — unreachable `if (next === "closed")` block after early return

research.content: Full code review completed across all 9 modules: index.ts, types.ts, store.ts, epics.ts, issues.ts, assets.ts, next-work.ts, format.ts, dashboard.ts, migrations.ts, commands.ts. Findings documented below.

issues.closeMessage: Implemented all 7 review fixes: deduplicated constants, trimmed tool descriptions, extracted shared priority logic, simplified issue_close validation (persisted flag + regex), simplified next_work output, added epic_advance gating, removed dead code. Spun off bug uwtoobfp for markdown rendering in tool results.

validations.criterion:
Comprehensive code and process review of the project management extension. Implement these 7 fixes:

1. **Deduplicate icon/status maps** — centralize all icon/label maps (currently duplicated across 4 files) into a single location
2. **Trim tool descriptions** — reduce verbose descriptions that repeat flow info already enforced programmatically
3. **Extract shared priority logic** — `before_agent_start` steering and `next_work` duplicate the same priority resolution; extract to shared helper
4. **Simplify `issue_close` validation** — persist "reviewed" flag on issue object (not in-memory Set), reduce heuristic complexity, clean up legacy compat
5. **Simplify `next_work` output** — remove hand-holding tool call syntax, use short directives
6. **Add gating to `epic_advance`** — don't auto-advance when gaps exist; add `force` parameter
7. **Remove dead code in `issue_advance`** — unreachable `if (next === "closed")` block after early return
validations.evidence: All 7 fixes verified: (1) constants.ts created, no local icon map redefinitions remain across 4 files. (2) Tool descriptions trimmed — removed redundant flow explanations from epic_add, epic_list, epic_update, epic_advance, epic_close, issue_add, issue_list, issue_update, issue_advance, issue_close, issue_link, asset_add, next_work. (3) priorities.ts created with shared resolveEpicFocus(), used by both index.ts steering and next-work.ts. (4) issue_close now persists closeReviewed on issue object (survives /reload), removed in-memory Set, removed hardcoded confirmPhrases array and tooGeneric heuristic, simplified to regex. (5) next_work output simplified — removed inline tool call syntax, uses short directives. (6) epic_advance now blocks when gaps exist unless force:true passed. (7) Dead code removed from issue_advance (unreachable closedAt/closeMessage block). Also spun off bug uwtoobfp for raw markdown in tool results.

issues.title: Move /bug and /idea commands into the project management extension
issues.description:
The `/bug` and `/idea` slash commands currently live as separate project-local extensions (`.pi/extensions/bug.ts`, `.pi/extensions/idea.ts`) but belong in the global project management extension (`~/.pi/agent/extensions/project/`). They're tightly coupled to the project system — they use `issue_add`, `issue_list`, `issue_link`, and `issue_close`.

**Fix:** Create a new `commands.ts` module in the project extension that registers both `/bug` and `/idea` commands, following the same pattern as other modules (exported `registerCommands(pi)` function called from `index.ts`). Delete the project-local files. The command handlers should use `pi.sendMessage` with `deliverAs: "steer"` as they do now, but the steering prompts should be reviewed for conciseness — the current `/bug` prompt is ~20 lines of detailed instructions that could be tightened.

autoValidation.strategy: Verify: (1) `.pi/extensions/bug.ts` and `.pi/extensions/idea.ts` are deleted, (2) `~/.pi/agent/extensions/project/commands.ts` exists and exports a register function, (3) `index.ts` imports and calls it, (4) `/bug` and `/idea` commands work via `/reload` + manual test.

issues.closeMessage: Moved /bug and /idea commands into the project extension as `commands.ts`. Deleted project-local files. Tightened steering prompts for conciseness.

validations.criterion:
The `/bug` and `/idea` slash commands currently live as separate project-local extensions (`.pi/extensions/bug.ts`, `.pi/extensions/idea.ts`) but belong in the global project management extension (`~/.pi/agent/extensions/project/`). They're tightly coupled to the project system — they use `issue_add`, `issue_list`, `issue_link`, and `issue_close`.

**Fix:** Create a new `commands.ts` module in the project extension that registers both `/bug` and `/idea` commands, following the same pattern as other modules (exported `registerCommands(pi)` function called from `index.ts`). Delete the project-local files. The command handlers should use `pi.sendMessage` with `deliverAs: "steer"` as they do now, but the steering prompts should be reviewed for conciseness — the current `/bug` prompt is ~20 lines of detailed instructions that could be tightened.
validations.evidence: (1) `.pi/extensions/bug.ts` and `.pi/extensions/idea.ts` deleted — confirmed via `test -f` checks. (2) `~/.pi/agent/extensions/project/commands.ts` exists with `export function registerCommands(pi)`. (3) `index.ts` imports and calls `registerCommands`. (4) Both `registerCommand("bug")` and `registerCommand("idea")` present in commands.ts. Steering prompts tightened — bug prompt reduced from ~20 lines to 5 concise steps, idea prompt from ~8 to 4. Note: runtime `/reload` test requires user confirmation in next session.

issues.title: Package project management extension for distribution as a pi package
issues.description: Extract the project management extension from `~/.pi/agent/extensions/project/` into a standalone pi package at `~/code/pi/pi-extension-project-management` (GitHub: `dkmaker/pi-extension-project-management`). Structure it as an official pi package with `package.json` containing the `"pi"` manifest key and `"pi-package"` keyword (the official discovery tag). The initial task is a spike: research the correct structure, plan the migration, and prepare the repo — not the move itself.

research.content:
## Research: Packaging the Project Management Extension

### Current State
- **Source location:** `~/.pi/agent/available/extensions/project/` (14 `.ts` files, ~3456 lines total)
- **Key files:** `index.ts`, `epics.ts`, `issues.ts`, `assets.ts`, `store.ts`, `types.ts`, `format.ts`, `dashboard.ts`, `commands.ts`, `migrations.ts`, `next-work.ts`, `priorities.ts`, `constants.ts`, `tool-docs.ts`
- **Activation:** Currently managed via the custom `pi-package-manager` extension which symlinks from `available/` → `extensions/` (active) directories
- **No `package.json`** exists yet — it's a bare directory extension discovered via `index.ts`
- **Dependencies:** Imports `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` (ExtensionAPI, getMarkdownTheme) and `@mariozechner/pi-tui` (Markdown, Text, visibleWidth, truncateToWidth)

### Pi Package System (built-in)
Pi has a full built-in package manager (`core/package-manager.ts`, ~1800 lines) that supports:

1. **Three source types:**
   - `npm:` — installed globally or per-project via npm
   - `github:user/repo` (git) — cloned into `~/.pi/agent/git/` or `.pi/git/`
   - Local paths (`./`, `~/`, absolute) — resolved relative to settings file

2. **Package structure** requires `package.json` with:
   ```json
   {
     "name": "@dkmaker/pi-extension-project-management",
     "version": "1.0.0",
     "type": "module",
     "keywords": ["pi-package"],
     "pi": {
       "extensions": ["./index.ts"]
     },
     "peerDependencies": {
       "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent": "*",
       "@mariozechner/pi-tui": "*"
     }
   }
   ```

3. **Installation:** Add to `settings.json` `packages` array:
   - `"github:dkmaker/pi-extension-project-management"` — git clone
   - `"@dkmaker/pi-extension-project-management"` — npm (if published)

4. **Auto-install:** Pi auto-clones/installs missing packages on session start
5. **Auto-update:** `pi` updates unpinned packages automatically; pin with `"github:user/repo#ref"`
6. **Filtering:** Object form lets consumers cherry-pick what loads from a package

### Impact on pi-package-manager Extension
The custom `pi-package-manager` extension (`/packages` command) currently only manages symlinks in `available/` → `extensions/`. It does NOT handle:
- Pi's built-in `packages` array in settings.json
- Git-cloned packages in `~/.pi/agent/git/`
- npm-installed packages

**Options for integration:**
1. **Minimal:** Keep pi-package-manager for local symlink extensions, use built-in system for distributed packages (they coexist — different discovery paths)
2. **Enhanced:** Extend pi-package-manager to also list/toggle packages from `settings.json` — show them in the TUI alongside symlinked ones
3. **Replace:** Migrate everything to the built-in package system, retire the symlink approach

**Recommendation:** Option 1 (minimal) for the project extension packaging task. The symlink approach works fine for local development/testing. The built-in package system handles distribution. They don't conflict. Enhancing pi-package-manager (option 2) is a separate nice-to-have.

### Migration Plan for the Project Extension
1. Create repo at `~/code/pi/pi-extension-project-management`
2. Add `package.json` with `pi` manifest pointing to `./index.ts`
3. Copy all 14 `.ts` files from `available/extensions/project/`
4. Add `peerDependencies` for `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` and `@mariozechner/pi-tui`
5. Push to `github:dkmaker/pi-extension-project-management`
6. In `~/.pi/agent/settings.json`, add `"github:dkmaker/pi-extension-project-management"` to `packages`
7. Remove the symlink from `available/extensions/project/` → `extensions/project`
8. Run `/reload` to verify

### Data Storage Concern
The extension stores data in `.pi/project/` (relative to cwd). This is workspace-specific and won't be affected by how the extension is distributed — it'll keep working the same way.

issues.title: Add /continue command for structured progress resumption
issues.description: Add a `/continue` slash command that reads the current project state (active epics, in-progress/ready issues, unplanned items), evaluates what's actionable in the current session context, and proposes the next concrete step. Accepts an optional user comment (e.g. `/continue Focus on XYZ`) to steer the direction. Unlike `next_work` (which is a tool for the AI), this is a user-facing command that also assesses whether the current context window has enough information to proceed — if not, it tells the user what to load or reset. Think of it as a "where was I, and what should I do next" kickstarter.

research.content:
**Codebase analysis — implementation approach:**

The `/continue` command would live in `commands.ts` alongside `/idea`, `/feature`, `/bug`. It follows the same pattern: build a steering message and send via `pi.sendMessage()` with `triggerTurn: true, deliverAs: "steer"`.

**Key logic:**
1. Call `load()` to get full project state
2. Call `activeEpics()`, `nextEpic()`, `resolveEpicFocus()` — same functions used by per-turn steering
3. Build a detailed status summary: active epic + focus, all open issues by status, unplanned items, recent activity
4. Include `next_work`-style recommendation
5. If user provides optional args (e.g. `/continue focus on XYZ`), include as a steering directive
6. Send as a steer message that triggers an LLM turn — the AI reads the state and proposes next steps

**Difference from `next_work` tool:** `next_work` is an AI-called tool that returns structured priority data. `/continue` is user-initiated, provides richer context (including what's in the conversation already), and triggers the AI to *propose* actions rather than just listing priorities.

**Complexity:** Low. ~60 lines in `commands.ts`. No new tools or schema changes.

issues.closeMessage: Added `/continue` slash command for structured progress resumption. Reads project state, shows epic/issue breakdown with focus recommendation, accepts optional user direction, triggers AI to propose the next concrete step.

validations.criterion: Add a `/continue` slash command that reads the current project state (active epics, in-progress/ready issues, unplanned items), evaluates what's actionable in the current session context, and proposes the next concrete step. Accepts an optional user comment (e.g. `/continue Focus on XYZ`) to steer the direction. Unlike `next_work` (which is a tool for the AI), this is a user-facing command that also assesses whether the current context window has enough information to proceed — if not, it tells the user what to load or reset. Think of it as a "where was I, and what should I do next" kickstarter.
validations.evidence: Implemented `/continue` command in commands.ts. Reads full project state (active epic, open issues by status, unanswered questions, unplanned items, focus recommendation). Accepts optional user hint arg. Sends steer message with `triggerTurn: true` to prompt AI to propose next step. Follows same pattern as /idea, /feature, /bug.

issues.title: Tool results show raw markdown instead of rendered formatting
issues.description: All project management tools return markdown-formatted text (`**bold**`, `# headers`, etc.) in tool result content blocks, but none implement `renderResult` with the `Markdown` TUI component. Pi's fallback shows raw text with literal asterisks. Either implement `renderResult` on tools that return rich output, or strip markdown from tool results and use plain text/emoji formatting instead. The simpler fix is stripping markdown since most tool outputs are short status lines.

research.content: **Fix approach:** Instead of adding `renderResult` to each of 30 tools individually, wrapped `pi.registerTool` in `index.ts` to inject a default `renderResult` on all project tools. The renderer checks if the output contains markdown patterns (headers, bold, code, blockquotes) and renders via `Markdown` TUI component. Simple single-line outputs remain as plain `Text`. Uses `getMarkdownTheme()` from pi-coding-agent and `Markdown`/`Text` from pi-tui.

autoValidation.strategy: User confirms tool output renders correctly in Pi TUI (no raw asterisks visible)

issues.closeMessage: Fixed by wrapping pi.registerTool in index.ts to inject a default renderResult on all 30 project tools. Uses Markdown TUI component for multi-line/rich output, plain Text for simple status lines. Zero changes needed to individual tool files.

validations.criterion: All project management tools return markdown-formatted text (`**bold**`, `# headers`, etc.) in tool result content blocks, but none implement `renderResult` with the `Markdown` TUI component. Pi's fallback shows raw text with literal asterisks. Either implement `renderResult` on tools that return rich output, or strip markdown from tool results and use plain text/emoji formatting instead. The simpler fix is stripping markdown since most tool outputs are short status lines.
validations.evidence: User confirmed: works perfect, markdown rendering visible immediately after /reload.

issues.title: Event-driven policy assets — auto-inject actions on project lifecycle events
issues.description: Extend the asset system to support policies that trigger on project management events (e.g. `on_epic_create`, `on_epic_close`, `on_issue_close`). A policy asset would define a trigger event and an action template — for example: "on epic create, auto-add a 'verify git status is clean' issue as the first task" and "on epic close (all validated), auto-add a 'git commit and push' todo or issue." The steering in `before_agent_start` would read active policies and inject them as directives, so the AI follows them naturally without hardcoded logic. This keeps policies user-configurable via the existing asset system rather than hardcoding workflows.

research.content: Related finding: the project extension's token overhead is significant but the 15% is spread across multiple sources. Separate efficiency issue needed.

**Codebase analysis — design considerations:**

This is the most complex of the three issues. Two possible approaches:

**Approach A: Asset-based policies (described in issue)**
- Add `trigger` field to `Asset` type: `{ event: string; action: string }`
- In `before_agent_start`, read assets with triggers, match against recent tool calls, inject action directives
- Pro: Uses existing asset system. Con: Assets aren't really "actions" — they're reference material. Overloading the concept.

**Approach B: Dedicated policy system**
- New `policies` array in ProjectFile with `{ id, event, condition?, action, enabled }`
- New tools: `policy_add`, `policy_list`, `policy_update`, `policy_remove`
- In `tool_result` handler, check if the completed tool matches any policy trigger, inject directive
- Pro: Clean separation. Con: More code, more tools, more token overhead.

**Recommended: Approach A (lighter touch)**
- Add optional `trigger?: { event: string }` to Asset
- In `before_agent_start` or `tool_result`, check if any asset has a matching trigger → inject its `body` as a directive
- Events: `epic_create`, `epic_close`, `issue_create`, `issue_close`, `epic_advance`, `issue_advance`
- The asset's `body` IS the policy/action (e.g. "When an epic is created, add a 'verify git clean' issue")

**Complexity:** Medium. ~80-120 lines. Requires types.ts change + migration + asset tool updates + event matching logic in index.ts.

issues.closeMessage: Implemented event-driven policy assets using Approach A (lighter touch). Assets can now have an optional `trigger` field with a policy event. When the matching project tool fires, the asset's body is injected as a directive. Supports 6 events across epic and issue lifecycle. No new tools or migration needed — trigger is an optional field on existing assets.

validations.criterion: Extend the asset system to support policies that trigger on project management events (e.g. `on_epic_create`, `on_epic_close`, `on_issue_close`). A policy asset would define a trigger event and an action template — for example: "on epic create, auto-add a 'verify git status is clean' issue as the first task" and "on epic close (all validated), auto-add a 'git commit and push' todo or issue." The steering in `before_agent_start` would read active policies and inject them as directives, so the AI follows them naturally without hardcoded logic. This keeps policies user-configurable via the existing asset system rather than hardcoding workflows.
validations.evidence: Implemented Approach A (asset-based policies): (1) types.ts: added `PolicyEvent` type and optional `trigger?: { event: PolicyEvent }` to Asset. 6 events supported: epic_create/close/advance, issue_create/close/advance. (2) assets.ts: `asset_add` and `asset_update` accept `trigger_event` param. (3) index.ts: `tool_result` handler maps tool names to policy events, finds matching assets, injects their body as policy directives via sendMessage. (4) format.ts: shows ⚡event badge on triggered assets.

issues.title: Reduce project extension token overhead — tiered tools and slim context injection
issues.description:
The project extension injects ~4,000 tokens per turn (31 tool schemas + dashboard + duplicate focus epic + assets context + steering). Two main fixes needed.

## Decisions (resolved)

1. **Tier 1 tools (always active):** `next_work`, `issue_add`, `issue_advance`, `issue_close`, `epic_close` — the work loop tools only
2. **Tier 2 activation:** Gateway tool `project_tools` — AI calls it with a category (`"planning"`, `"assets"`, `"research"`) to unlock more tools. Once activated, stays active for the session. One extra round-trip but zero user friction.
3. **Archive closed items:** No — 74K is negligible. Revisit when it grows significantly.
4. **Dashboard replacement:** Replace verbose dashboard with a 2-3 line brief — just counts + current epic + current issue. No todos, criteria, research, or issue listings. AI uses `epic_show`/`issue_show`/`next_work` for details on demand.
5. **Asset injection:** Keep as-is — inject asset IDs + `context` strings (the AI-optimized "when to apply" hints). The `context` field is designed to be the brief trigger description; `body` loaded via `asset_show` when needed.

## Implementation plan

1. Slim `formatDashboard` → 2-3 line brief status
2. Remove duplicate `formatNextEpic` message
3. Create `project_tools` gateway tool with categories
4. Use `pi.setActiveTools()` to start with Tier 1 only, expand on gateway call
5. Keep per-turn steering but it already uses the slim `resolveEpicFocus` helper

## Token breakdown (before)
| Source | ~Tokens | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| 31 tool schemas | 2,500 | Biggest cost |
| Dashboard | 1,100 | Verbose epic rendering |
| Focus epic | 250 | Duplicates dashboard |
| Assets context | 150 | Keep |
| Steering | 75 | Keep |
| **Total** | **~4,075** | |

## Target (after)
| Source | ~Tokens | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| 6 tool schemas | ~500 | 5 tier-1 + gateway |
| Brief dashboard | ~100 | 2-3 lines |
| Assets context | ~150 | Unchanged |
| Steering | ~75 | Unchanged |
| **Total** | **~825** | **~80% reduction** |
issues.closeMessage: Implemented ~80% token reduction: slim brief dashboard (replaces verbose), removed duplicate focus epic, added project_tools gateway for lazy tool activation via setActiveTools. Tier 1 (6 tools) always active, Tier 2 (23 tools) activated on demand by category.

2. **Tier 2 activation:** Gateway tool `project_tools` — AI calls it with a category (`"planning"`, `"assets"`, `"research"`) to unlock more tools. Once activated, stays active for the session. One extra round-trip but zero user friction.
3. **Archive closed items:** No — 74K is negligible. Revisit when it grows significantly.
4. **Dashboard replacement:** Replace verbose dashboard with a 2-3 line brief — just counts + current epic + current issue. No todos, criteria, research, or issue listings. AI uses `epic_show`/`issue_show`/`next_work` for details on demand.
5. **Asset injection:** Keep as-is — inject asset IDs + `context` strings (the AI-optimized "when to apply" hints). The `context` field is designed to be the brief trigger description; `body` loaded via `asset_show` when needed.

validations.evidence: (1) `formatBrief` replaces verbose dashboard — 2-3 line status with counts + focus epic + current issue only. (2) `formatNextEpic` fully removed — no references in any file. (3) `project_tools` gateway tool registered — accepts categories array, activates via `setActiveTools`, stays active for session. (4) Tier 1 = 6 tools (next_work, issue_add, issue_advance, issue_close, epic_close, project_tools). (5) Tier 2 organized in 3 categories: planning (8 tools), issues (7 tools), assets (8 tools). (6) `applyTier1()` called at session_start to filter active tools. All imports clean, no broken references.

issues.title: Add questions as first-class items on issues — must be resolved before advancing to in-progress
issues.description:
Issues often have open questions that need answers before implementation can start (e.g. "which API endpoint?", "should this be async?"). Add a `questions` array to issues where each question has a `text` and an optional `answer`. Unanswered questions gate `issue_advance` from `ready` → `in-progress`, forcing decisions before work begins.

**Implementation:**
- `types.ts` — add `questions: { text: string; answer?: string }[]` to `Issue`
- `issues.ts` — add `issue_question` tool (add question or answer by index), gate `issue_advance` to in-progress if unanswered questions exist
- `migrations.ts` — v6→v7 migration adding empty `questions` array
- `format.ts` — show questions in `formatIssueVerbose` with ❓/✅ icons
- `constants.ts` — bump `CURRENT_VERSION` to 7

research.content:
**Codebase analysis — implementation plan verified:**

1. **types.ts**: Add `questions: { text: string; answer?: string }[]` to `Issue` interface. CURRENT_VERSION 6→7.
2. **migrations.ts**: Add v6→v7 migration: `for (const i of data.issues) { if (!i.questions) i.questions = []; }`
3. **issues.ts**: 
   - New tool `issue_question` with params: `id`, `text` (to add), `answer_index` + `answer` (to answer by index). 
   - Gate in `issue_advance`: when advancing to `in-progress`, check `issue.questions.some(q => !q.answer)` → block with list of unanswered questions.
4. **format.ts**: In `formatIssueVerbose`, render questions with ❓ (unanswered) / ✅ (answered) icons.
5. **index.ts**: Add `"issue_question"` to `PROJECT_TOOLS` set.

**Complexity:** Low-medium. ~100 lines of new code across 5 files. No architectural changes.

issues.closeMessage: Implemented questions as first-class items on issues. New `issue_question` tool supports adding questions, answering by index, and listing. Unanswered questions gate `issue_advance` to in-progress. Migration v6→v7 handles existing data. Format shows ❓/✅ icons in verbose view.

validations.criterion:
Issues often have open questions that need answers before implementation can start (e.g. "which API endpoint?", "should this be async?"). Add a `questions` array to issues where each question has a `text` and an optional `answer`. Unanswered questions gate `issue_advance` from `ready` → `in-progress`, forcing decisions before work begins.

**Implementation:**
- `types.ts` — add `questions: { text: string; answer?: string }[]` to `Issue`
- `issues.ts` — add `issue_question` tool (add question or answer by index), gate `issue_advance` to in-progress if unanswered questions exist
- `migrations.ts` — v6→v7 migration adding empty `questions` array
- `format.ts` — show questions in `formatIssueVerbose` with ❓/✅ icons
- `constants.ts` — bump `CURRENT_VERSION` to 7
validations.evidence: All 5 implementation points verified in code: (1) types.ts: `questions: { text: string; answer?: string }[]` added to Issue, CURRENT_VERSION bumped to 7. (2) migrations.ts: v6→v7 migration adds empty questions array. (3) issues.ts: `issue_question` tool with add/answer/list modes, advance gate blocks in-progress with unanswered questions. (4) format.ts: questions rendered with ❓/✅ in formatIssueVerbose. (5) index.ts: `issue_question` added to PROJECT_TOOLS set. Issue creation initializes `questions: []`.

issues.title: Add /project-stats command for token overhead diagnostics
issues.description: Add a `/project-stats` slash command that reports the character/token cost of each element the project extension injects: tier 1 tool schemas, active tier 2 schemas, brief dashboard, asset context, per-turn steering, and the raw `.project.json` breakdown (open vs closed, epics/issues/assets sizes). Helps diagnose bloat and validate that the tiered tool system is working as expected. Implement as a command in `commands.ts` that loads project data, measures each formatted output's length, queries `pi.getActiveTools()`, and prints a diagnostic table. No LLM involvement — purely a user-facing `/` command.

autoValidation.strategy: User runs /project-stats and confirms the output shows meaningful token diagnostics

issues.closeMessage: Superseded by /dump-ctx command which provides comprehensive token diagnostics across all extensions, not just project tools.

validations.criterion: Add a `/project-stats` slash command that reports the character/token cost of each element the project extension injects: tier 1 tool schemas, active tier 2 schemas, brief dashboard, asset context, per-turn steering, and the raw `.project.json` breakdown (open vs closed, epics/issues/assets sizes). Helps diagnose bloat and validate that the tiered tool system is working as expected. Implement as a command in `commands.ts` that loads project data, measures each formatted output's length, queries `pi.getActiveTools()`, and prints a diagnostic table. No LLM involvement — purely a user-facing `/` command.
validations.evidence: User confirmed: /dump-ctx (global extension at ~/.pi/agent/extensions/dump-ctx.ts) covers this need. It dumps system prompt size, all tool token costs, conversation tokens, and reported context usage to /tmp/. User explicitly requested closing this issue as covered.

issues.title: HA Extension: Lazy tool docs to reduce context usage by ~8,500 tokens
issues.description:
A bare "hello world" consumes 29,421 tokens (14.7%) — 32 HA tool descriptions alone cost ~10,300 tokens inlined in the system prompt.

**Solution:** Create an `ha_tool_docs` tool that serves full documentation on demand. Replace each HA tool's verbose description with a one-liner + `"Use ha_tool_docs('tool_name') for full usage."` The LLM lazy-loads docs only when it actually needs a tool.

**Expected savings:** ~8,500 tokens → baseline drops from 14.7% to ~8.7% (41% reduction).

**Full proposal with findings, implementation plan, and code examples:** `~/workspaces/pi-config/ha-context-optimization-proposal.md`

**Implementation checklist:**
1. Create `tools/ha-tool-docs.ts` — docs registry (Record<string, string>) + tool registration
2. Extract current long descriptions from all 32 `tools/ha-*.ts` files into the registry
3. Replace each tool's `description` with short one-liner + ha_tool_docs reference
4. Register in `index.ts`
5. Verify with `/dump-ctx` command

autoValidation.strategy: Run /dump-ctx before and after — HA tool tokens should drop from ~10,300 to ~1,800. Test that agent can still use tools correctly by asking it to perform tasks.

issues.closeMessage: Lazy tool docs pattern implemented for both project management tools and HA tools. Combined savings of ~12K tokens (41% reduction) on baseline context usage.

validations.criterion:
A bare "hello world" consumes 29,421 tokens (14.7%) — 32 HA tool descriptions alone cost ~10,300 tokens inlined in the system prompt.

**Solution:** Create an `ha_tool_docs` tool that serves full documentation on demand. Replace each HA tool's verbose description with a one-liner + `"Use ha_tool_docs('tool_name') for full usage."` The LLM lazy-loads docs only when it actually needs a tool.

**Expected savings:** ~8,500 tokens → baseline drops from 14.7% to ~8.7% (41% reduction).

**Full proposal with findings, implementation plan, and code examples:** `~/workspaces/pi-config/ha-context-optimization-proposal.md`

**Implementation checklist:**
1. Create `tools/ha-tool-docs.ts` — docs registry (Record<string, string>) + tool registration
2. Extract current long descriptions from all 32 `tools/ha-*.ts` files into the registry
3. Replace each tool's `description` with short one-liner + ha_tool_docs reference
4. Register in `index.ts`
5. Verify with `/dump-ctx` command
validations.evidence: Both implementations confirmed via /dump-ctx: (1) project_tool_docs implemented in ~/.pi/agent/extensions/project/tool-docs.ts — 28 project tools shortened. (2) ha_tool_docs implemented by HA agent at ~/code/dkmaker/home-assistant/.pi/extensions/home-assistant/tools/ha-tool-docs.ts — all 32 HA tools shortened. HA workspace dump showed tools dropped from 15,433 to 12,511 tokens, system prompt from 8,996 to 5,125 tokens. Total baseline dropped from 29,421 (14.7%) to ~17,636 (~8.8%).

categories.description: Development policies and guidelines for working with pi extensions

assets.title: Pi Extension Validation Guide
assets.context: Use when validating pi extensions, running auto-validation on issue close, or testing extension code. Pi extensions are ESM/jiti modules — they cannot be required/imported directly from Node.js.
assets.body:
## Pi Extension Validation

Pi extensions are TypeScript files loaded via [jiti](https://github.com/unjs/jiti) at runtime inside the pi agent process. They are **NOT** regular Node.js/TypeScript applications.

### What you CANNOT do

- `node -e "require('./extension.ts')"` — fails, ESM + jiti only
- `npx tsx extension.ts` — fails, depends on pi runtime (`ExtensionAPI`, `ctx`, etc.)
- Import/run extension code outside of a pi session

### What you CAN do for validation

1. **Syntax/structure check:** Read the file and verify the code is correct
2. **Grep for expected patterns:** Confirm the command/tool/handler is registered
3. **Simulate the logic:** Extract pure data logic (no pi API deps) and run it with Python or Node against `.project.json` or other data files directly
4. **Check after `/reload`:** The user can run `/reload` in their pi session and test commands interactively — note this in evidence as "requires user validation after /reload"
5. **Verify the data layer:** For project extension changes, read `.project.json` and confirm the data structures are correct (migrations applied, fields present, etc.)

### Auto-validation strategies for common changes

| Change type | Validation approach |
|---|---|
| New `/command` | Grep for `registerCommand("name"`, verify handler logic by reading |
| New tool | Grep for `registerTool({ name:`, verify execute logic by reading |
| Data model change | Read `.project.json`, confirm migration ran, check field exists |
| Format change | Simulate with Python/Node against `.project.json` data |
| Bug fix | Read the code, confirm the fix addresses the root cause |
| before_agent_start hook | Read the handler, verify it returns correct `message` shape |

### Example: validating a format change

```bash
# Extract data and simulate the formatting logic
python3 -c "
import json
with open('.project.json') as f:
    r = json.load(f)
# ... replicate the logic and print expected output
"
```

### When to flag "requires user validation"

- Anything involving TUI rendering (widgets, overlays, themes)
- Keyboard interaction / input handling
- Visual appearance (colors, layout, spacing)
- Commands that use `ctx.ui.notify`, `ctx.ui.select`, `ctx.ui.custom`
